title: Visualizing Video Data
concept: Video data is stored numerically as a 4D tensor with dimensions: Frames x Channels x Height x Width.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What makes the temporal dimension different from spatial dimensions?

options.text: It flows sequentially in one direction (time)
It only exists in grayscale images

exercises.explain: Yes! Time has a strict sequential order that dictates the flow of actions.

cards.title: Thinking in 4D
cards.body: In machine learning, a single color photo is represented as a 3D grid: Height, Width, and 3 Color Channels (Red, Green, Blue). But what happens when you stack multiple photos to make a video? You get a 4-dimensional block of numbers!

cards.title: The 4D Video Tensor
cards.body:
A video is stored in a deep learning model as a 4D tensor. The standard shape is (F, C, H, W) or (F, H, W, C), where:
• F = number of frames (time)
• C = channels (RGB)
• H = height
• W = width

cards.title: A loaf of sliced bread
cards.body: Think of a video tensor as a loaf of sliced bread. A single slice is 2D (height and width). The three channels are the ingredients mixed in. The entire loaf is the 4D tensor: multiple slices stacked in order. Each slice represents a point in time.

cards.title: Calculating tensor sizes
cards.body:
Let's calculate the size of a 3-second video at 10 FPS, with 100x100 RGB frames.
• Frames (F) = 30
• Channels (C) = 3
• Height (H) = 100, Width (W) = 100
Total values: 30 × 3 × 100 × 100 = 900,000 numbers!

cards.title: Not a video file format
cards.body: You might think the neural network processes MP4 or AVI files directly. It doesn't! Video files are heavily compressed. Before feeding a video to a model, it must be decoded into raw numeric arrays of pixel intensities.

cards.title: Image Tensor vs. Video Tensor
cards.body: Comparing the tensor layouts of image and video data:

right.text:
Dimensions: (F, C, H, W)
e.g. (16, 3, 224, 224)
Stack of 16 image matrices

exercises.prompt: What does the 'F' stand for in a (F, C, H, W) video tensor?
exercises.explain: Correct! 'F' stands for the number of frames in the video sequence.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An MP4 file must be decompressed and decoded into raw pixel tensors before a neural network can analyze it.
exercises.explain: True. Neural networks operate on raw numerical pixel arrays, not compressed video file wrappers like MP4.

exercises.sentence: While a static color image is represented as a 3D tensor, a color video is represented as a ___ tensor.
exercises.explain: A video adds the frame/time dimension, increasing the tensor rank from 3D to 4D.

exercises.prompt: Which tensor shape represents 8 frames of 224x224 RGB video?
exercises.explain: The correct format is (Frames, Channels, Height, Width), which maps to (8, 3, 224, 224).

exercises.prompt: How many total numerical values are in a grayscale (1 channel) video tensor that is 5 frames long, with a height of 10 pixels and a width of 10 pixels?
exercises.explain: Exactly! Calculated as: 5 (frames) × 1 (channel) × 10 (height) × 10 (width) = 500 values. Next: what happens if we try to process these frames one by one?
